text: This is a ten-year-old boy. He is diagnosed to have acute appendicitis.
His total count is fourteen thousand and we performed laparoscopic appendectomy.
The surgeon uses two Alice forceps to grasp the skin on either side of the inferior crease of the umbilicus.
A small stab incision is made in the inferior crease of the umbilicus.
The surgeon lifts the abdominal wall and inserts a Veress needle through the stab incision at the inferior crease of the umbilicus.
The surgeon performs the irrigation test, followed by the suction test, and then the hanging drop test, likely to assess the integrity and function of the surgical site.
Set the encephalator in pediatric mode.
The surgeon attaches a tube to the varus needle, which is inserted into the abdominal wall.
The flow of gas should be zero point one liter per year of the age.
The insufflator is activated, causing the abdomen to uniformly distend as gas is introduced into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon completes the insufflation process, then removes the varus needle from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon enlarges the stab incision to an 11 millimeter size, preparing the site for further surgical access.
The surgeon carefully introduces the trocar into the abdominal cavity, holding the tip with the index finger positioned to guard the instrument.
and listen for the hissing noise of air.
The surgeon connects the insufflation tube to the trocar, ensuring the tube is securely attached for continued abdominal insufflation.
The surgeon introduces the first umbilical port and inserts the telescope through it, providing a visual view of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs an initial diagnostic laparoscopy, visually inspecting the abdominal cavity through the laparoscopic camera.
The surgeon introduces a 7 mm working port through the left iliac fossa, preparing for further laparoscopic access.
The surgeon introduces a 5 mm working port through the suprapubic area.
The external camera view shows the locations of the laparoscopic ports, including the suprapubic, left iliac fossa, and umbilical port sites.
The laparoscopic view reveals the cecum, ileum, ileocecal junction, and an inflamed appendix, with visible signs of inflammation around the appendix.
The inflamed appendix is visibly adherent to the lateral abdominal wall.
The surgeon performs a gentle blunt dissection to separate the inflamed appendix from the lateral abdominal wall, using careful manipulation to detach the adherent tissue.
Because it is acute case, there is no dense adhesions.
The surgeon performs suction irrigation to clear the surgical field, removing fluids and debris to improve visibility of the inflamed appendix and surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery and scissors to separate the mesoappendix from the inflamed appendix, with visible cauterization and cutting of tissue.
The surgeon cauterizes the mesoappendix close to the inflamed appendix using a bipolar cautery device, minimizing bleeding due to the small size of the vessels.
The surgeon lifts the appendix to prevent the diathermy from contacting surrounding bowel loops, ensuring safe cauterization.
In pediatric appendix, you will not have much of mesoappendix because of less fat.
The surgeon uses suction irrigation to remove minor oozing from the surgical field, ensuring clear visibility for the procedure.
The surgeon skeletonizes the appendix down to its base, using irrigation and suction to clear the surgical field.

The surgeon performs skeletonization at the base of the appendix, ensuring dissection reaches the cecum to prevent incomplete appendectomy. The mesoappendix is positioned away, allowing the appendix to be twisted for better access.
The surgeon has nearly reached the base of the appendix, with the structure visibly isolated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the tinea coli, a visible anatomical landmark, which guides the approach to the base of the appendix.
The surgeon introduces an extracorporeal endo loop and secures it at the base of the appendix using a knot pusher, completing the ligation process.
The surgeon introduces a second endo loop and secures it three millimeters distal to the first loop at the base of the appendix.
The surgeon introduces a third endoloop and secures it six millimeters distal to the second endoloop on the appendix.
It helps to prevent spillage of fecolith if it is present.
The surgeon cuts the appendix between the second and third endoloops, which are visibly secured around the appendix.
The surgeon ensures a minimum three millimeter stem is maintained at the base of the appendix to securely hold the knot, preventing it from becoming loose.
The surgeon removes the appendix through a seven millimeter port, following the placement of endoloops to secure the appendiceal stump.
The surgeon performs a thorough wash of the surgical field, including the right anterior flank (RAF), Morrison's pouch, and the pelvis, ensuring all areas are clean and free of debris.
Thank you for watching.
